content.text:
initial research and setup for the project.

we are going to create a plugin for Obsidian which turns its editor into a true outliner experience. by true outliner I mean the functionality and UX similar to 

* Logseq (opensource, almost perfect, but stuck for years in a major rewrite)
* outl (new, opensource, similar to Logseq, very promising)
* Tana Outliner (recently split up into a separate app)
* Workflowy
* Roam research (well-known)
* Orca note (mentions bi-directional outlining, [https://orca-studio.com/orcanote](https://orca-studio.com/orcanote/))
* see the general info: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Outliner
* do your own research for more concrete details about the UI, the functionality and the mechanics of a good outliner app.

---

after doing the research and getting a good grasp of what the general idea is, let's look at the existing Obsidian plugins:

* https://community.obsidian.md/plugins/obsidian-outliner
   * a good improvement to the default Obsidian experience, improved the writing with nested lists a lot, 
   * but it's still a flat text under the hood, with some cursor tricks bolted on top
      * selecting nodes reveals that very clearly: you can't select node-per-node, only the raw text behind, together with uneven indentation, etc.
         * a clumsy select and delete will leave a broken outline tree with messed up structure and indentation
      * it's still possible to just add a newline and start writing normal flat markdown, next to a list/outline
   * it doesn't "enforce" the structure in any way
   * https://community.obsidian.md/plugins/bullet
      * a fork of the outliner plugin
* https://github.com/vslinko/obsidian-zoom
   * common outline feature, to zoom into a subtree
   * not essential for me, but a nice to have
* https://community.obsidian.md/plugins/draggable-list-items
   * this functionality is probably included in the outliner plugin
* bi-directionality of outline: backlinks view can benefit a lot from relying on the structure of the quoting documents (this is really good in Logseq)
   * https://community.obsidian.md/plugins/better-search-views (not outliner-specific, but tries to improve the structure display in the search/backilns views)
   * https://community.obsidian.md/plugins/influx (a direct attempt, but in a stale/abandoned state)
   * https://community.obsidian.md/plugins/coalesce (a newer attempt at better backlinks display)

it's important not to mix up the Outliner concept with the "sidebar outline" in Obsidian (or any other editor) which shows a TOC/minimam structure of a document. they are related in a way, but we are focusing on the document's structure itself and the editing experience, not an extra UI on a side.

---

next, I want you to check my experiment with a TUI outliner editor: `tui-outliner-multiline`. it's a raw prototype and isn't a reference of the desired implementation, but there might be parts of it that are valuable. in particular, I want you to inspect the `specs/` folder (core-outliner, multiline edits), the specs and the user scenario descriptions. that project was built using spec-driven development (with SpecKit) and we are going to use SDD here as well (but with OpenSpec tooling). take into account that this is just an inspiration and some of the described scenarios/behaviors in that project are biased towards the TUI-specifics. 

---

I want you to conduct a broad and deep research, document thoroughly the understanding of what the goal is, the examples of good/bad, the known issues, the opportunities, etc. etc. anything that comes up as relevant.

the goal is to create a true outliner experience in Obsidian in a form of a comprehensive coherent plugin with a perfect scorecard (security, guidelines, no private APIs or hacks, etc.). this is an additional point of research: is it possible to achieve the desired experience with the existing plugin APIs in Obsdian?

---

if anything is unclear, or questions/doubts arise during/after the research, don't make any assumptions, stop and ask me directly. there is some space for interpretation and ambiguity, different preferences, etc. so I want to make sure we have a full alignment before committing to any plan.
I'll start by looking at what's already in this repo (INIT.md, openspec/), then explore your TUI outliner experiment, and then do the external research.

1	# TUI Outliner
2	
3	[![Test](https://github.com/laughedelic/tui-outliner/actions/workflows/test.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/laughedelic/tui-outliner/actions/workflows/test.yml)
4	[![codecov](https://codecov.io/github/laughedelic/tui-outliner/graph/badge.svg?token=E1BT6H0HJC)](https://codecov.io/github/laughedelic/tui-outliner)
5	
6	> [!WARNING]
7	> This project is in active development. It is an experiment and isn't meant for real-world use. 
8	
9	A simple outliner for terminal. This is an experiment in building terminal UIs with [OpenTUI](https://github.com/sst/opentui) and React, using spec-driven development (via [Spec Kit](https://github.com/github/spec-kit)).
10	
11	## Quick Start
12	
13	### Prerequisites
14	
15	- **Bun** >=1.2.0 ([install](https://bun.sh/docs/installation))
16	- **Zig** (latest stable) ([install](https://ziglang.org/learn/getting-started/)) - required for OpenTUI builds
17	
18	### Installation
19	
20	```bash
21	# Clone the repository
22	git clone https://github.com/laughedelic/tui-outliner.git
23	cd tui-outliner
24	
25	# Install dependencies
26	bun install
27	
28	# Run the application
29	bun run dev
30	```
31	
32	### Usage
33	
34	Open an existing outline:
35	
36	```bash
37	bun run dev path/to/outline.json
38	```
39	
40	Open an outline in read-only mode (prevents all edits):
41	
42	```bash
43	# Using long flag
44	bun run dev --read-only path/to/outline.json
45	
46	# Using short flag
47	bun run dev -r path/to/outline.json
48	```
49	
50	The `--read-only` (or `-r`) flag opens the outline in read-only mode for this session only. This is useful for browsing reference materials, documentation, or any hierarchical data without risk of accidental modifications. Read-only mode is not persisted to the file.
51	
52	## Keyboard Shortcuts
53	
54	### Navigation
55	
56	| Key       | Action                         |
57	| --------- | ------------------------------ |
58	| `↑` / `k` | Move to previous node          |
59	| `↓` / `j` | Move to next node              |
60	| `←` / `h` | Move to parent node            |
61	| `→` / `l` | Expand and move to first child |
62	| `Home`    | Jump to first node             |
63	| `End`     | Jump to last node              |
64	
65	### Editing
66	
67	| Key                       | Action                                                |
68	| ------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- |
69	| `i`                       | Edit at beginning of text (vim-style)                 |
70	| `a`                       | Edit at end of text (vim-style)                       |
71	| `Enter` (nav)             | Edit current node                                     |
72	| `Enter` (edit)            | Create sibling below                                  |
73	| `Shift+Enter` (edit)      | Insert newline (multiline text)                       |
74	| `o`                       | Create sibling below (or first child if has children) |
75	| `O`                       | Create sibling above                                  |
76	| `Backspace` (edit, empty) | Delete node and move to previous                      |
77	| `Backspace` (nav)         | Delete node (confirm if has children)                 |
78	| `Ctrl+D`                  | Force delete node with children                       |
79	| `Escape`                  | Exit edit mode                                        |
80	
81	### Structure
82	
83	| Key                     | Action                                       |
84	| ----------------------- | -------------------------------------------- |
85	| `Tab` / `Shift+→`       | Indent node (make child of previous sibling) |
86	| `Shift+Tab` / `Shift+←` | Dedent node (promote to parent's sibling)    |
87	| `Shift+J` / `Shift+↓`   | Move node down                               |
88	| `Shift+K` / `Shift+↑`   | Move node up                                 |
89	
90	### Visibility
91	
92	| Key     | Action             |
93	| ------- | ------------------ |
94	| `Space` | Toggle fold/unfold |
95	
96	### Zoom
97	
98	| Key               | Action                 |
99	| ----------------- | ---------------------- |
100	| `.`               | Zoom into focused node |
101	| `,`               | Zoom out to root       |
102	| `←` (on top node) | Zoom up one level      |
103	
104	### Undo/Redo
105	
106	| Key                       | Action                  |
107	| ------------------------- | ----------------------- |
108	| `Ctrl+Z` / `u`            | Undo last change        |
109	| `Ctrl+Shift+Z` / `Ctrl+R` | Redo last undone change |
110	
111	**Note**: Undo/redo supports all operations: text edits, node creation/deletion, moves, indent/dedent, fold/unfold, and zoom. History is limited to 50 steps and is cleared when loading a new file.
112	
113	### File Operations
114	
115	| Key                              | Action        |
116	| -------------------------------- | ------------- |
117	| `Ctrl+S`                         | Save manually |
118	| Auto-save after 2s of inactivity |
119	
120	## File Format
121	
122	Outlines are saved as JSON files with the following structure:
123	
124	```json
125	{
126	  "version": "1.0.0",
127	  "root": {
128	    "id": "root",
129	    "text": "",
130	    "children": [
131	      {
132	        "id": "uuid-1",
133	        "text": "First item",
134	        "children": [],
135	        "isFolded": false
136	      }
137	    ]
138	  }
139	}
140	```
141	
142	**Note**: Read-only mode is controlled via the `--read-only` CLI flag and is not persisted in the file format.
143	
144	### Backups
145	
146	Backups are automatically created as `.backup-TIMESTAMP.json` before each save.
147	
148	## Examples
149	
150	### Organizing Meeting Notes
151	
152	```
153	• Meeting Notes
154	  • Attendees
155	    • Alice
156	    • Bob
157	  • Discussion Points
158	    • Project Timeline
159	      • Phase 1: Complete
160	      • Phase 2: In Progress
161	    • Budget Review
162	  • Action Items
163	    • Follow up with client
164	    • Update documentation
165	```
166	
167	### Project Planning
168	
169	```
170	• Project Outline
171	  • Research Phase
172	    • Literature Review [folded]
173	    • Competitor Analysis [folded]
174	  • Development
175	    • MVP Features
176	      • User Authentication
177	      • Core Functionality
178	    • Testing
179	  • Launch
180	```
181	
182	### Read-Only Reference Documents
183	
184	Use the `--read-only` flag to view reference materials without accidentally editing them:
185	
186	```bash
187	# Open any file in read-only mode
188	bun run dev --read-only docs/project-spec.json
189	```
190	
191	Example read-only outline (navigation works, editing is blocked):
192	
193	```
194	• Project Specification [READ-ONLY]
195	  • Requirements
196	    • User must be able to navigate with arrow keys
197	    • Support for 1000+ nodes
198	  • Architecture
199	    • Frontend: React + OpenTUI
200	    • Backend: File-based storage
201	  • Constraints
202	    • Must work offline
203	    • No external dependencies
204	```
205	
206	### Multiline Text Nodes
207	
208	Use Shift+Enter to create nodes with multiline content:
209	
210	```
211	• Code Examples
212	  • Python Function
213	    function greet(name):
214	        """Greet a user by name"""
215	        return f"Hello, {name}!"
216	  • Meeting Notes
217	    Attendees: Alice, Bob, Charlie
218	
219	    Discussion:
220	    - Project timeline reviewed
221	    - Budget approved
222	
223	    Action items:
224	    - Follow up with stakeholders
225	    - Schedule next meeting
226	  • Documentation
227	    Installation:
228	    1. Install Bun
229	    2. Run: bun install
230	    3. Start: bun run dev
231	```
232	
233	## Development
234	
235	See [AGENTS.md](AGENTS.md) for AI agent development instructions and [specs/001-core-outliner-mvp/quickstart.md](specs/001-core-outliner-mvp/quickstart.md) for detailed development guide.
236	
237	### Running Tests
238	
239	```bash
240	# Run all tests
241	bun test
242	
243	# Type checking
244	bun run typecheck
245	
246	# Manual testing
247	bun run dev
248	```
249	
250	### Project Structure
251	
252	```
253	tui-outliner/
254	├── src/
255	│   ├── components/     # React components
256	│   ├── hooks/         # Custom React hooks
257	│   ├── lib/           # Core logic (pure functions)
258	│   └── types/         # TypeScript types
259	├── tests/
260	│   ├── unit/          # Unit tests
261	│   ├── integration/   # Integration tests
262	│   └── fixtures/      # Test data
263	└── specs/             # Feature specifications
264	```
265	
266	## Credits
267	
268	Built with:
269	- [OpenTUI](https://github.com/sst/opentui) - Terminal UI framework
270	- [React](https://react.dev/) - UI library
271	- [Bun](https://bun.sh/) - JavaScript runtime
272	- [SpecKit](https://github.com/github/spec-kit) - Specification-driven development

1	# Feature Specification: Core Outliner MVP
2	
3	**Feature Branch**: `001-core-outliner-mvp`  
4	**Created**: 2025-10-26  
5	**Status**: Draft  
6	**Input**: User description: "I'm building a TUI outliner app with OpenTUI and React. Focus on Phase 1: Core Outliner (MVP). Key requirements: Keyboard-first navigation, Tree-based outline structure, Inline text editing, File persistence (JSON), Fold/unfold nodes, Zoom navigation with breadcrumbs"
7	
8	## User Scenarios & Testing *(mandatory)*
9	
10	<!--
11	  IMPORTANT: User stories should be PRIORITIZED as user journeys ordered by importance.
12	  Each user story/journey must be INDEPENDENTLY TESTABLE - meaning if you implement just ONE of them,
13	  you should still have a viable MVP (Minimum Viable Product) that delivers value.
14	  
15	  Assign priorities (P1, P2, P3, etc.) to each story, where P1 is the most critical.
16	  Think of each story as a standalone slice of functionality that can be:
17	  - Developed independently
18	  - Tested independently
19	  - Deployed independently
20	  - Demonstrated to users independently
21	-->
22	
23	### User Story 1 - Navigate and Browse Outline (Priority: P1)
24	
25	A user opens an outline file and navigates through nested items using only keyboard controls. They can move between nodes, see the current focus clearly, and understand the outline structure at a glance.
26	
27	**Why this priority**: Navigation is the foundation of all outliner functionality. Without smooth, intuitive navigation, no other feature matters. This is the most frequent operation users will perform.
28	
29	**Independent Test**: Can be fully tested by loading a sample outline with 20+ nested nodes and using arrow keys to navigate. Delivers immediate value by allowing users to read and browse their outlines.
30	
31	**Acceptance Scenarios**:
32	
33	1. **Given** an outline is loaded with multiple nodes, **When** user presses Down Arrow, **Then** focus moves to the next visible node
34	2. **Given** focus is on a node with children, **When** user presses Right Arrow, **Then** the node expands and focus moves to first child
35	3. **Given** focus is on a child node, **When** user presses Left Arrow, **Then** focus moves to the parent node
36	4. **Given** an outline with 100+ nodes is loaded, **When** user navigates with arrow keys, **Then** response time is under 16ms (60fps)
37	5. **Given** user is navigating, **When** focus changes, **Then** the focused node is clearly highlighted with visual distinction
38	
39	---
40	
41	### User Story 2 - Create and Edit Content (Priority: P1)
42	
43	A user creates new outline nodes and edits their text content inline. They can add nodes at any position in the tree, type text naturally, and see their changes immediately.
44	
45	**Why this priority**: Content creation is the second most fundamental operation. Users need to input and modify information to make the outliner useful for note-taking.
46	
47	**Independent Test**: Can be fully tested by entering edit mode (pressing 'i' or 'a'), typing text, pressing Enter to create new nodes, and verifying changes persist in the data structure. Delivers value by allowing users to capture and organize thoughts.
48	
49	**Acceptance Scenarios**:
50	
51	1. **Given** focus is on any node, **When** user presses 'i', **Then** edit mode activates with cursor at the beginning of the text
52	2. **Given** focus is on any node, **When** user presses 'a', **Then** edit mode activates with cursor at the end of the text
53	3. **Given** user is in edit mode, **When** user presses Enter, **Then** a new sibling node is created below and edit mode continues on the new node
54	4. **Given** user is in navigation mode, **When** user presses Enter, **Then** edit mode activates on the current node
55	5. **Given** user is in navigation mode on a node without children, **When** user presses 'o', **Then** a new sibling node is created below and edit mode activates on it
56	6. **Given** user is in navigation mode on a node with children, **When** user presses 'o', **Then** a new child node is created as the first child and edit mode activates on it
57	7. **Given** user is in navigation mode, **When** user presses 'O', **Then** a new sibling node is created above and edit mode activates on it
58	8. **Given** user is editing an empty node, **When** user presses Backspace, **Then** the node is deleted and cursor moves to the end of the previous visible node
59	9. **Given** user is in navigation mode, **When** user presses Backspace on any node, **Then** the node is deleted (with confirmation for nodes with children)
60	10. **Given** user is editing a node, **When** user types text, **Then** text appears with no perceptible lag
61	11. **Given** user is editing a node, **When** user presses Escape, **Then** editing mode exits and changes are saved

65	### User Story 3 - Organize Structure (Priority: P2)
66	
67	A user reorganizes their outline by indenting, dedenting, and moving nodes up/down within their sibling list. They can change both the hierarchy and order to reflect their thinking, and see the structural changes immediately.
68	
69	**Why this priority**: Structural editing is what makes an outliner powerful compared to plain lists. While not needed for initial capture, it's essential for organizing information effectively. Moving nodes up/down is critical because dedenting often changes node order in unexpected ways—explicit move operations give users precise control over both hierarchy and sequence.
70	
71	**Independent Test**: Can be fully tested by selecting nodes and using Tab/Shift+Tab or Shift+Right/Shift+Left to change hierarchy levels, and Shift+J/Shift+K or Shift+Down/Shift+Up to reorder siblings. Delivers value by allowing users to structure and sequence their information hierarchically.
72	
73	**Acceptance Scenarios**:
74	
75	1. **Given** focus is on a node with a sibling above it, **When** user presses Tab or Shift+Right, **Then** the node becomes a child of the previous sibling
76	2. **Given** focus is on a child node, **When** user presses Shift+Tab or Shift+Left, **Then** the node becomes a sibling of its parent
77	3. **Given** user indents/dedents a node, **When** the operation completes, **Then** the visual hierarchy updates with proper indentation
78	4. **Given** a node has children, **When** user indents it under a new parent, **Then** all children move with it maintaining their structure
79	5. **Given** focus is on a node with a sibling above it, **When** user presses Shift+K or Shift+Up, **Then** the node (with all its children) swaps position with the previous sibling
80	6. **Given** focus is on a node with a sibling below it, **When** user presses Shift+J or Shift+Down, **Then** the node (with all its children) swaps position with the next sibling
81	7. **Given** focus is on the first child of a parent whose parent has a previous sibling, **When** user presses Shift+K or Shift+Up, **Then** the node moves to become the last child of the parent's previous sibling
82	8. **Given** focus is on the last child of a parent whose parent has a next sibling, **When** user presses Shift+J or Shift+Down, **Then** the node moves to become the first child of the parent's next sibling
83	9. **Given** focus is on the first child of a parent with no previous sibling, **When** user presses Shift+K or Shift+Up, **Then** nothing happens (operation is ignored gracefully)
84	10. **Given** focus is on the last child of a parent with no next sibling, **When** user presses Shift+J or Shift+Down, **Then** nothing happens (operation is ignored gracefully)
85	11. **Given** user moves a node up or down, **When** the operation completes, **Then** focus remains on the moved node and visual order updates immediately

89	### User Story 4 - Manage Visibility (Priority: P2)
90	
91	A user collapses and expands nodes to focus on relevant parts of their outline. They can hide details when not needed and expand them when required, making large outlines manageable.
92	
93	**Why this priority**: Folding is critical for working with large outlines but isn't needed for small initial captures. It becomes important as outlines grow beyond 20-30 nodes.

98	
99	1. **Given** focus is on a node with children, **When** user presses Space, **Then** the children become hidden and a fold indicator appears
100	2. **Given** focus is on a folded node, **When** user presses Space again, **Then** the children become visible
101	3. **Given** multiple nodes are folded, **When** user navigates with arrow keys, **Then** only visible nodes are accessible
102	4. **Given** user folds a node, **When** navigation skips over hidden children, **Then** the behavior feels predictable and intuitive

106	### User Story 5 - Focus on Subtrees (Priority: P2)
107	
108	A user zooms into a specific node, making it temporarily the root of their view. They can see breadcrumbs showing their location and easily zoom back out to parent contexts. Navigation within the zoomed view works the same as at the root level, maintaining consistency.
109	
110	**Why this priority**: Zoom navigation is valuable for deep outlines but not essential for basic functionality. It enhances usability once users have substantial content.
111	
112	**Independent Test**: Can be fully tested by selecting a node and triggering zoom, then verifying breadcrumb display, navigation within zoomed view, and zoom-out functionality. Delivers value by providing focus in complex outlines.
113	
114	**Acceptance Scenarios**:
115	
116	1. **Given** focus is on any node (except the currently zoomed node), **When** user presses `.` (period), **Then** that node becomes the visible root, breadcrumbs show the ancestral path (excluding the zoomed node itself), and focus moves to the first visible child (or the node itself if no children)
117	2. **Given** focus is on the currently zoomed node (the top node in the zoomed view), **When** user presses `.` (period), **Then** the zoom-in operation is ignored (no action) to prevent confusing double-zoom behavior
118	3. **Given** user is zoomed into a node at any depth, **When** user presses `,` (comma), **Then** the view returns to the root level (all the way out) and focus moves to the node that was the zoom container
119	4. **Given** user is zoomed in and focused on a child node within the zoomed view, **When** user presses Left arrow, **Then** focus moves to the parent node (standard navigation behavior)
120	5. **Given** user is zoomed in and focused on the top node of the zoomed view (the node we zoomed into), **When** user presses Left arrow, **Then** the view zooms up one level in the hierarchy and focus moves to the revealed parent node
121	6. **Given** user is at the root level (not zoomed), **When** user presses Left arrow on a top-level node, **Then** the operation is ignored gracefully (no zoom up possible)
122	7. **Given** user is zoomed in multiple levels, **When** breadcrumbs are displayed, **Then** they show the full ancestral path (excluding the zoomed node itself), or "[root]" if zoomed into a top-level node
123	8. **Given** user is navigating within a zoomed view, **When** using arrow keys on child nodes, **Then** navigation works identically to root-level navigation (Left = parent, Right = first child, Up/Down = siblings)
124	9. **Given** user zooms out from any depth, **When** returning to root, **Then** focus is set on the top node of the previous zoomed view, regardless of where focus was within that zoomed view
125	10. **Given** user is zoomed in and focused on any node, **When** navigating with Up/Down arrows, **Then** movement is restricted to visible nodes within the current zoom context

129	### User Story 6 - Save and Load Work (Priority: P1)
130	
131	A user saves their outline to a file and loads it later, with confidence that no data will be lost or corrupted. The system auto-saves periodically and indicates when changes are unsaved.
132	
133	**Why this priority**: Data persistence is non-negotiable for any productivity tool. Without reliable save/load, the tool is unusable. Auto-save provides additional safety.
134	
135	**Independent Test**: Can be fully tested by creating an outline, saving it, restarting the app, and verifying all content and structure are preserved. Delivers value by making the tool practical for real work.
136	
137	**Acceptance Scenarios**:
138	
139	1. **Given** user has created/modified an outline, **When** user presses Ctrl+S, **Then** the outline is saved to a JSON file atomically (temp file then move)
140	2. **Given** an outline file exists, **When** user opens the app with that file path, **Then** the complete outline structure is restored
141	3. **Given** user makes changes, **When** 2 seconds pass with no further edits, **Then** the outline auto-saves automatically
142	4. **Given** unsaved changes exist, **When** displayed in the status bar, **Then** a clear indicator shows "unsaved changes"
143	5. **Given** a save operation fails, **When** the failure occurs, **Then** in-memory state is preserved and user sees a clear error message
144	6. **Given** user attempts to quit with unsaved changes, **When** quit is triggered, **Then** user is prompted to save first

165	### Edge Cases
166	
167	- What happens when a file cannot be loaded (corrupted JSON, wrong format, missing file)?
168	  - System displays clear error message
169	  - Offers to create a new empty outline or retry with a different file
170	  - Never crashes or shows stack traces to user
171	
172	- What happens when the outline has 500+ nodes?
173	  - Navigation remains responsive (<16ms per keypress)
174	  - Folding/unfolding is performant
175	  - File save/load stays under performance targets (100ms save, 200ms load)
176	
177	- What happens when terminal is resized during use?
178	  - Layout adjusts gracefully
179	  - No visual corruption
180	  - Focused node remains visible
181	
182	- What happens when user tries to indent a node that has no previous sibling?
183	  - Operation is ignored or shows brief feedback
184	  - No error crash
185	
186	- What happens when user saves to a location without write permissions?
187	  - Clear error message displayed
188	  - Option to save to different location
189	  - In-memory data preserved
190	
191	- What happens when user presses an unmapped key combination?
192	  - Ignored gracefully, no error
193	  - Status bar might show "unknown command" briefly
194	
195	- What happens with the root node itself?
196	  - Root node (with ID "root" and empty text) is never displayed to users
197	  - Only the children of root are visible in the outline
198	  - This prevents confusing empty nodes at the top of the outline
199	
200	- What happens when user tries to zoom in while already focused on the zoomed node?
201	  - Operation is ignored gracefully (no visual change)
202	  - Prevents confusing double-zoom behavior where zooming out would require two operations
203	  - Status bar may briefly show "Already zoomed into this node"
204	
205	- What happens when user presses Left arrow on different nodes within a zoomed view?
206	  - If focused on the top node (the zoomed container): zooms up one level, reveals parent
207	  - If focused on any child node: performs normal navigation (moves focus to parent node)
208	  - This maintains consistency with standard navigation behavior
209	
210	- What happens when user zooms out from multiple levels deep?
211	  - Single `,` (comma) keypress returns all the way to root level
212	  - Focus moves to the top node of the previous zoom container
213	  - For gradual zoom-up, user can use Left arrow on top nodes repeatedly
214	
215	- What happens when breadcrumbs are displayed for a top-level zoom?
216	  - Breadcrumbs show "[root]" to indicate zoomed into a direct child of root
217	  - This clarifies position in hierarchy even when no intermediate ancestors exist
218	
219	- What happens to focus after zooming in?
220	  - Focus moves to first visible child of the zoomed node (if children exist)
221	  - Focus stays on the zoomed node if it has no children
222	  - This allows immediate interaction with the zoomed content
223	
224	- What happens to focus after zooming out?
225	  - Focus always moves to the top node that was the previous zoom container
226	  - This occurs regardless of where focus was within the zoomed view
227	  - Provides predictable landing point after zoom out

347	## Design Notes
348	
349	### Zoom Navigation Mental Model
350	
351	The zoom navigation feature allows users to temporarily treat any node as the "root" of their view, focusing on that node's subtree. This design balances powerful navigation capabilities with predictable, consistent behavior.
352	
353	#### Key Design Decisions
354	
355	**1. Breadcrumbs Show Ancestors, Not the Zoomed Node**
356	
357	When zoomed into a node, breadcrumbs display the path TO that node (its ancestors), not including the node itself. This is because:
358	- The zoomed node is already the visible "root" of the current view
359	- Including it in breadcrumbs would be redundant and confusing
360	- Breadcrumbs represent the path you traveled to get here, not where you are
361	
362	Example:
363	```
364	Hierarchy:        A → B → C → D (focused)
365	Zoomed into D:    Breadcrumbs show "A > B > C"
366	                  View shows D and its children
367	```
368	
369	For top-level nodes: `Breadcrumbs show "[root]"` to indicate position
370	
371	**2. Three Different Zoom Operations with Distinct Behaviors**
372	
373	The system provides three zoom-related operations, each with a specific purpose:
374	
375	| Key                | Operation         | Behavior                                                                                                                                | Use Case                                         |
376	| ------------------ | ----------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------ |
377	| `.` (period)       | Zoom In           | Focus on selected node's subtree. Ignored if already zoomed into that node. Focus moves to first child (or node itself if no children). | "I want to focus on this section"                |
378	| `,` (comma)        | Zoom Out          | Return to root level in one jump, regardless of depth. Focus moves to previous zoom container.                                          | "I'm done with this subtree, show me everything" |
379	| `Left` on top node | Zoom Up One Level | Move up one level in hierarchy (like climbing breadcrumbs). Focus moves to revealed parent.                                             | "I want to see the parent context"               |
380	
381	**3. Left Arrow Behavior Depends on Context**
382	
383	The Left arrow key has dual behavior based on focus position:
384	- **On child nodes**: Standard navigation (move focus to parent node within current view)
385	- **On top node**: Zoom up one level (reveal parent in hierarchy)
386	
387	This design maintains navigation consistency while providing intuitive zoom control. The user's mental model: "Left always means 'go to parent'" — but when you're at the top of the current view, the parent is in a broader context.
388	
389	**4. Zoom Out Goes All The Way to Root**
390	
391	Comma (`,`) always returns to root level, not just one level up. Rationale:
392	- Simpler mental model (one key = root level)
393	- Avoids confusion about zoom stack depth
394	- For gradual navigation, use Left arrow on top nodes
395	- Most common workflow: zoom in to focus → work → zoom out to see everything
396	
397	**5. Focus Management on Zoom Operations**
398	
399	Focus behavior is carefully designed for predictability:
400	
401	- **Zoom In (`.`)**: Focus moves to first child (or node itself if no children)
402	  - Rationale: User wants to immediately interact with the content
403	  - Prevents focusing on the container node which is now the "root"
404	
405	- **Zoom Out (`,`)**: Focus moves to the top node of the previous zoom container
406	  - Rationale: Provides consistent landing point
407	  - User knows where they'll be after zoom out
408	  - Doesn't matter where focus was within zoomed view
409	
410	- **Zoom Up One Level (Left on top)**: Focus moves to the revealed parent
411	  - Rationale: Consistent with "Left = parent" navigation
412	  - Parent becomes focused when it enters the view
413	
414	**6. Preventing Confusing Double-Zoom**
415	
416	Zoom in (`.`) is ignored when focused on the currently zoomed node (top node of view). This prevents:
417	- Accidentally zooming into the same node twice
418	- Confusing "zoom out once = stay here, zoom out twice = actually go back" behavior
419	- Users expecting consistent zoom in/out pairing
420	
421	**7. Navigation Within Zoomed View Matches Root Behavior**
422	
423	All navigation (Up/Down/Left/Right) within a zoomed view works identically to navigation at root level:
424	- Left on child = parent
425	- Right on parent = first child  
426	- Up/Down = siblings
427	- Space = fold/unfold
428	
429	This consistency means users don't need to learn different navigation rules for zoomed contexts.
430	
431	#### Implementation Notes
432	
433	The zoom stack internally stores the complete path including the zoomed node (needed for `getVisibleNodes` to work correctly), but the breadcrumbs display logic excludes the last item when rendering. This separation of concerns allows the internal representation to be complete while the UI presentation is clean.

457	- JSON is an acceptable file format for outline storage (human-readable, widely supported)
458	- Single-file outlining is sufficient for Phase 1 (multiple file support deferred to Phase 2)
459	- Undo/redo can be deferred to Phase 2 without breaking core usability
460	- Search functionality can be deferred to Phase 2
461	- Mouse interactions are optional enhancements, not required for Phase 1
462	- Single-line text within nodes is sufficient for Phase 1 (multi-line support deferred to Phase 2+ based on research findings)
463	- Standard keyboard shortcuts (Ctrl+S for save, Escape to exit modes, Enter to create) are intuitive for target users
464	
465	## Out of Scope (Phase 1)
466	
467	- Undo/redo functionality (Phase 2)
468	- Multiple file support and file switching (Phase 2)
469	- Search and filtering (Phase 2)
470	- Wiki-links and backlinks (Phase 2)
471	- Multi-line text within nodes (Phase 2+)
472	- Export to other formats (Markdown, HTML, etc.)
473	- Cloud sync or collaboration features
474	- Plugin system or extensibility
475	- Themes or extensive customization
476	- AI-powered features
477	- Mobile or GUI versions
478	
479	## Keyboard Shortcuts Reference
480	
481	**Navigation Mode**:
482	- `Up/Down Arrow` or `k/j` - Move between visible nodes
483	- `Left Arrow` or `h` - Move to parent node
484	- `Right Arrow` or `l` - Expand node and move to first child
485	- `Home` - Jump to first visible node
486	- `End` - Jump to last visible node
487	
488	**Editing**:
489	- `i` - Enter edit mode with cursor at beginning of text (vim-style)
490	- `a` - Enter edit mode with cursor at end of text (vim-style)
491	- `Enter` (navigation mode) - Enter edit mode on current node
492	- `Enter` (edit mode) - Create new sibling node below and continue editing
493	- `o` (navigation mode) - Create new sibling node below and enter edit mode (vim-style)
494	- `O` (navigation mode) - Create new sibling node above and enter edit mode (vim-style)
495	- `Backspace` (edit mode on empty node) - Delete node and move to end of previous node
496	- `Backspace` (navigation mode) - Delete node (with confirmation for nodes with children)
497	- `Ctrl+D` (navigation mode) - Force delete node with children
498	- `Escape` - Exit edit mode and save changes
499	
500	**Structure**:
501	- `Tab` or `Shift+Right` - Indent node (make child of previous sibling)
502	- `Shift+Tab` or `Shift+Left` - Dedent node (promote to parent's sibling)
503	- `Shift+J` or `Shift+Down` - Move node down within sibling list
504	- `Shift+K` or `Shift+Up` - Move node up within sibling list
505	
506	**Visibility**:
507	- `Space` - Toggle fold/unfold node
508	
509	**Zoom**:
510	- `.` (period) - Zoom into focused node
511	- `,` (comma) - Zoom out to parent level
512	
513	**File Operations**:
514	- `Ctrl+S` - Manual save
515	- Auto-save triggers after 2 seconds of inactivity
516

1	# OpenTUI Development Learnings
2	
3	**Project**: TUI Outliner MVP  
4	**Framework**: OpenTUI (@opentui/react ^0.1.30)  
5	**Completed**: 2025-11-01  
6	**Purpose**: Document patterns, performance insights, and limitations discovered during development
7	
8	---
9	
10	## Table of Contents
11	
12	1. [OpenTUI Architecture Patterns](#opentui-architecture-patterns)
13	2. [Keyboard Event Handling](#keyboard-event-handling)
14	3. [Focus Management](#focus-management)
15	4. [Layout & Rendering](#layout--rendering)
16	5. [Performance Optimization](#performance-optimization)
17	6. [State Management](#state-management)
18	7. [Testing Strategies](#testing-strategies)
19	8. [Limitations & Workarounds](#limitations--workarounds)
20	9. [Best Practices](#best-practices)
21	10. [Recommendations for Future OpenTUI Projects](#recommendations)
22	
23	---
24	
25	## OpenTUI Architecture Patterns
26	
27	### What OpenTUI Is
28	
29	OpenTUI is a TUI framework with a Zig-powered rendering backend and optional React integration. You write 100% TypeScript/React code - Zig is only needed as a build dependency for OpenTUI's internal optimizations.
30	
31	**Key Components**:
32	- `@opentui/react`: React integration with JSX components
33	- `@opentui/core`: Low-level renderables and CLI renderer
34	- Yoga Layout: CSS Flexbox for terminal layouts
35	- Zig Backend: High-performance buffer rendering (abstracted away)
36	
37	### Development Setup
38	
39	```bash
40	# Prerequisites
41	bun >=1.2.0  # JavaScript runtime
42	zig          # Required for OpenTUI native module compilation
43	
44	# Installation
45	bun add @opentui/react @opentui/core react
46	```
47	
48	> [!NOTE]
49	> Zig must be available during `bun install` for OpenTUI to build its native modules. You never write Zig code yourself.
50	
51	### Project Structure Pattern
52	
53	```
54	src/
55	├── components/    # React components using OpenTUI primitives
56	├── hooks/         # Custom hooks for state management
57	├── lib/           # Pure functions (tree ops, navigation, file I/O)
58	├── types/         # TypeScript interfaces
59	└── index.tsx      # Entry point
60	```
61	
62	**Separation of Concerns**:
63	- **lib/**: Pure, framework-agnostic logic (testable in isolation)
64	- **components/**: OpenTUI React components (UI rendering)
65	- **hooks/**: React hooks bridging lib/ and components/
66	
67	This separation makes unit testing straightforward and keeps UI logic minimal.
68	
69	---
70	
71	## Keyboard Event Handling
72	
73	### Pattern: Custom Hook for Keyboard Input
74	
75	**Implementation**:
76	```typescript
77	// src/hooks/useNavigation.ts
78	import { useKeyboard } from '@opentui/react';
79	
80	export function useNavigation(onNavigate: (direction: string) => void) {
81	  useKeyboard((key) => {
82	    // Arrow keys
83	    if (key.name === 'up' || key.name === 'k') {
84	      onNavigate('up');
85	      return true; // Mark as handled
86	    }
87	    
88	    // Ctrl modifier
89	    if (key.ctrl && key.name === 's') {
90	      saveFile();
91	      return true;
92	    }
93	    
94	    return false; // Not handled, allow propagation
95	  });
96	}
97	```
98	
99	**Key Insights**:
100	
101	1. **Return `true` to consume events**: Prevents propagation to other handlers
102	2. **Return `false` to propagate**: Allows global handlers to process
103	3. **Keyboard events are global**: Only the focused component receives input
104	4. **Modifier keys work**: `key.ctrl`, `key.shift`, `key.alt`, `key.meta`
105	
106	**Terminal-Specific Caveats**:
107	- Some terminals intercept `Ctrl+Arrow` for system shortcuts
108	- `Shift+Up/Down` may conflict with terminal selection on some platforms
109	
110	---
111	
112	## Focus Management
113	
114	### Pattern: Ref-Based Focus Control
115	
116	OpenTUI has a global focus model: only one component can be focused at a time.
117	
118	**Implementation**:
119	```typescript
120	const scrollboxRef = useRef<ScrollBoxRenderable | null>(null);
121	
122	useEffect(() => {
123	  scrollboxRef.current?.focus();
124	}, []);
125	```
126	
127	**Key Insights**:
128	
129	1. **Focus is exclusive**: Setting focus on component A removes focus from component B
130	2. **No visual focus indicator by default**: You must implement your own (highlight, background color)
131	3. **Focus persists across renders**: Unless explicitly changed
132	4. **Keyboard events only go to focused component**: Design accordingly
133	
134	### Focus Management Strategy
135	
136	For complex UIs, track focus state separately from OpenTUI:
137	
138	```typescript
139	const [focusedNodeId, setFocusedNodeId] = useState<string>('root');
140	
141	// Render with conditional styling
142	<node isFocused={node.id === focusedNodeId} />
143	```
144	
145	This approach gives you more control and makes state testable.
146	
147	---
148	
149	## Layout & Rendering
150	
151	### Pattern: Flexbox-Based Layout
152	
153	OpenTUI uses Yoga (Facebook's Flexbox implementation) for layout.
154	
155	**Basic Layout**:
156	```tsx
157	<box
158	  style={{
159	    rootOptions: {
160	      flexDirection: 'column',
161	      flexGrow: 1,
162	    }
163	  }}
164	>
165	  {/* Children */}
166	</box>
167	```
168	
169	**Scrollable Container**:
170	```tsx
171	<scrollbox
172	  ref={scrollboxRef}
173	  style={{
174	    rootOptions: {
175	      flexGrow: 1,
176	    }
177	  }}
178	>
179	  {items.map(item => <text key={item.id}>{item.text}</text>)}
180	</scrollbox>
181	```
182	
183	### Key Layout Properties
184	
185	| Property          | Values                        | Use Case                         |
186	| ----------------- | ----------------------------- | -------------------------------- |
187	| `flexDirection`   | `column`, `row`               | Stack vertically or horizontally |
188	| `flexGrow`        | `0`, `1`, `2`...              | Proportional space distribution  |
189	| `flexShrink`      | `0`, `1`                      | Allow shrinking when space tight |
190	| `width`, `height` | `auto`, `number`, `%`         | Fixed or proportional sizing     |
191	| `overflow`        | `visible`, `hidden`, `scroll` | Content overflow behavior        |
192	
193	### Rendering Performance
194	
195	**Critical Finding**: Rendering is extremely fast even with large lists.
196	
197	**Benchmark Results**:
198	- 500 visible nodes: <1ms render time
199	- 1000 visible nodes: <2ms render time
200	- 120 nodes (typical): <0.05ms
201	
202	**Optimization Tips**:
203	- Use `key` prop for list items (standard React optimization)
204	- Avoid inline style objects (memoize with `useMemo`)
205	- Keep component tree shallow when possible
206	- OpenTUI's reconciliation is efficient - don't over-optimize
207	
208	---
209	
210	## Performance Optimization
211	
212	### Pattern: Separate Logic from Rendering
213	
214	**Anti-Pattern** (Slow):
215	```typescript
216	// Computing visible nodes on every render
217	function OutlineView() {
218	  const visibleNodes = computeVisibleNodes(root, zoomStack); // ❌ Recalculates every render
219	  return visibleNodes.map(node => <Node {...node} />);
220	}
221	```
222	
223	**Best Practice** (Fast):
224	```typescript
225	// Pure function in lib/, memoized result
226	function OutlineView({ root, zoomStack }) {
227	  const visibleNodes = useMemo(
228	    () => getVisibleNodes(root, zoomStack),
229	    [root, zoomStack] // Only recompute when these change
230	  );
231	  
232	  return visibleNodes.map(node => <Node key={node.id} {...node} />);
233	}
234	```
235	
236	### Navigation Performance
237	
238	**Achieved Performance**:
239	- Target: <16ms (60fps)
240	- Actual: 0.001-0.003ms average
241	- 500+ nodes: Still <1ms
242	
243	**Key Techniques**:
244	1. **Precompute visible nodes**: Don't traverse tree on every keypress
245	2. **Use node maps**: O(1) lookup instead of tree traversal
246	3. **Memoize computations**: Cache results until state changes
247	4. **Avoid re-renders**: Use `React.memo` for static components
248	
249	### File I/O Performance
250	
251	**Atomic Save Pattern**:
252	```typescript
253	// Write to temp file, then atomic rename
254	const tempPath = `${filePath}.tmp`;
255	await Bun.write(tempPath, JSON.stringify(data));
256	await rename(tempPath, filePath);
257	```
258	
259	**Performance Results**:
260	- 1000-node save: <50ms (target: <100ms)
261	- 1000-node load: <100ms (target: <200ms)
262	
263	**Optimization**:
264	- Use Bun's native `Bun.write()` (faster than `fs.writeFile`)
265	- Validate before save to avoid wasted I/O
266	- Create backups asynchronously (don't block UI)
267	
268	---
269	
270	## State Management
271	
272	### Pattern: Custom Hooks + Pure Functions
273	
274	**Architecture**:
275	```
276	User Input → Hook → Pure Function → New State → Re-render
277	```
278	
279	**Example**:
280	```typescript
281	// Pure function (lib/tree-operations.ts)
282	export function insertNode(
283	  root: OutlineNode,
284	  targetId: string,
285	  text: string
286	): OutlineNode {
287	  // Returns NEW tree (immutable)
288	}
289	
290	// Custom hook (hooks/useOutlineOperations.ts)
291	export function useOutlineOperations(root, setRoot) {
292	  const handleInsert = useCallback((text: string) => {
293	    const newRoot = insertNode(root, focusedId, text);
294	    setRoot(newRoot);
295	  }, [root, focusedId]);
296	  
297	  return { handleInsert };
298	}
299	
300	// Component (components/App.tsx)
301	const { handleInsert } = useOutlineOperations(root, setRoot);
302	```
303	
304	**Benefits**:
305	- Pure functions are easily testable (unit tests)
306	- Hooks handle React lifecycle (useCallback, useEffect)
307	- Components stay thin (just rendering)
308	
309	### Immutable Data Pattern
310	
311	**Critical**: All tree operations return NEW trees, never mutate in place.
312	
313	```typescript
314	// ✅ Correct: Returns new tree
315	export function editNodeText(root: OutlineNode, id: string, text: string): OutlineNode {
316	  if (root.id === id) {
317	    return { ...root, text }; // New object
318	  }
319	  return {
320	    ...root,
321	    children: root.children.map(child => editNodeText(child, id, text))
322	  };
323	}
324	
325	// ❌ Wrong: Mutates in place
326	export function editNodeText(root: OutlineNode, id: string, text: string): void {
327	  if (root.id === id) {
328	    root.text = text; // Mutation breaks React
329	  }
330	}
331	```
332	
333	**Why Immutability**:
334	- React detects changes via reference equality
335	- Enables undo/redo (Phase 2 feature)
336	- Prevents subtle bugs from shared references
337	- Makes state changes auditable
338	
339	---
340	
341	## Testing Strategies
342	
343	### Unit Tests for Pure Functions
344	
345	**Pattern**: Test logic independently of OpenTUI/React.
346	
347	```typescript
348	// tests/unit/tree-operations.test.ts
349	import { describe, test, expect } from 'bun:test';
350	import { insertNode, deleteNode } from '@/lib/tree-operations';
351	
352	test('insertNode adds sibling after target', () => {
353	  const root = createTestTree();
354	  const newRoot = insertNode(root, 'node-1', 'New Node', 'after');
355	  
356	  expect(newRoot.children).toHaveLength(2);
357	  expect(newRoot.children[1].text).toBe('New Node');
358	});
359	```
360	
361	**Benefits**:
362	- Fast execution (no rendering)
363	- Easy to write (no mocking)
364	- High coverage with minimal code
365	
366	### Integration Tests for Workflows
367	
368	**Pattern**: Test multi-step user workflows with actual tree state.
369	
370	```typescript
371	// tests/integration/navigation-workflow.test.ts
372	test('should navigate down through visible nodes', () => {
373	  const tree = createMultiNodeTree();
374	  const visible = getVisibleNodes(tree, []);
375	  
376	  let currentId = visible[0].id;
377	  currentId = getNextNodeId(visible, currentId, 'down');
378	  
379	  expect(currentId).toBe(visible[1].id);
380	});
381	```
382	
383	### Performance Benchmarks
384	
385	**Pattern**: Validate performance targets with automated tests.
386	
387	```typescript
388	// tests/integration/performance.test.ts
389	test('navigation should take <16ms for 100-node outline', () => {
390	  const tree = generateLargeTree(120);
391	  const visible = getVisibleNodes(tree, []);
392	  
393	  const iterations = 100;
394	  const start = performance.now();
395	  
396	  for (let i = 0; i < iterations; i++) {
397	    getNextNodeId(visible, visible[0].id, 'down');
398	  }
399	  
400	  const end = performance.now();
401	  const avgTime = (end - start) / iterations;
402	  
403	  expect(avgTime).toBeLessThan(16);
404	});
405	```
406	
407	**Testing Coverage**:
408	- 208 tests total
409	- 144 unit tests (pure functions)
410	- 64 integration tests (workflows, performance)
411	- 100% coverage on critical paths (navigation, file I/O, validation)
412	
413	---
414	
415	## Limitations & Workarounds
416	
417	### Limitation 1: No Multi-Line Text Input
418	
419	**Issue**: OpenTUI text input is single-line only. Enter key fires as a key event, not newline.
420	
421	**Current Workaround**: Enter creates new sibling node (acceptable for Phase 1).
422	
423	**Future Solution**: Multi-line editing via modal or external editor integration (Phase 2+).
424	
425	### Limitation 2: No Mouse Support (Yet)
426	
427	**Issue**: OpenTUI doesn't expose mouse events in current version.
428	
429	**Workaround**: Design 100% keyboard-first. Mouse support can be added later if OpenTUI adds it.
430	
431	**Impact**: None - our constitution prioritizes keyboard anyway.
432	
433	### Limitation 3: Limited Terminal Auto-Scrolling
434	
435	**Issue**: OpenTUI doesn't auto-scroll focused items into view.
436	
437	**Workaround**: Implement manual scroll management with `useEffect`:
438	
439	```typescript
440	useEffect(() => {
441	  const scrollbox = scrollboxRef.current;
442	  if (!scrollbox || focusedIndex === -1) return;
443	  
444	  const viewportHeight = scrollbox.viewport.height;
445	  const currentScrollTop = scrollbox.scrollTop;
446	  
447	  if (focusedIndex < currentScrollTop + 2) {
448	    scrollbox.scrollTo(Math.max(0, focusedIndex - 2));
449	  } else if (focusedIndex > currentScrollTop + viewportHeight - 3) {
450	    scrollbox.scrollTo(focusedIndex - viewportHeight + 3);
451	  }
452	}, [focusedIndex]);
453	```
454	
455	### Limitation 4: Terminal Compatibility Variance
456	
457	**Issue**: Different terminals handle keys differently (especially Ctrl/Shift combos).
458	
459	**Workaround**: Provide multiple key bindings for critical actions:
460	- Indent: `Tab` OR `Shift+Right`
461	- Move up: `Shift+K` OR `Shift+Up`
462	
463	**Testing**: Validate in iTerm2, Terminal.app, and Ghostty minimum.
464	
465	### Limitation 5: No Built-In Undo/Redo
466	
467	**Issue**: OpenTUI doesn't provide state management or history.
468	
469	**Workaround**: Implement manually with immutable state history (Phase 2 feature).
470	
471	```typescript
472	// Future pattern for undo/redo
473	const [history, setHistory] = useState<OutlineNode[]>([initialRoot]);
474	const [historyIndex, setHistoryIndex] = useState(0);
475	
476	function undo() {
477	  if (historyIndex > 0) {
478	    setHistoryIndex(historyIndex - 1);
479	    setRoot(history[historyIndex - 1]);
480	  }
481	}
482	```
483	
484	---
485	
486	## Best Practices
487	
488	### 1. Separate Pure Logic from UI
489	
490	**Why**: Testability, reusability, maintainability.
491	
492	**How**:
493	- `/lib/`: Pure functions (tree ops, navigation, validation)
494	- `/hooks/`: React hooks for side effects
495	- `/components/`: Minimal rendering logic
496	
497	### 2. Use TypeScript Strict Mode
498	
499	**Why**: Catch bugs at compile time, not runtime.
500	
501	**How**:
502	```json
503	// tsconfig.json
504	{
505	  "compilerOptions": {
506	    "strict": true,
507	    "noUncheckedIndexedAccess": true
508	  }
509	}
510	```
511	
512	**Note**: Test files may have strict warnings (e.g., `tree.children[0]` possibly undefined). This is acceptable if tests validate behavior correctly.
513	
514	### 3. Memoize Expensive Computations
515	
516	**Why**: OpenTUI re-renders on state change - avoid redundant work.
517	
518	**How**:
519	```typescript
520	const visibleNodes = useMemo(
521	  () => getVisibleNodes(root, zoomStack),
522	  [root, zoomStack]
523	);
524	```
525	
526	### 4. Validate Data Before Persistence
527	
528	**Why**: Prevents corruption, catches bugs early.
529	
530	**How**:
531	```typescript
532	export async function saveOutline(path: string, root: OutlineNode) {
533	  const errors = validateTree(root);
534	  if (errors.length > 0) {
535	    throw new Error(`Invalid tree: ${errors.join(', ')}`);
536	  }
537	  
538	  // Atomic write: temp → rename
539	  const tempPath = `${path}.tmp`;
540	  await Bun.write(tempPath, JSON.stringify({ version: '1.0.0', root }));
541	  await rename(tempPath, path);
542	}
543	```
544	
545	### 5. Test Performance with Realistic Data
546	
547	**Why**: Small test data hides performance problems.
548	
549	**How**:
550	```bash
551	# Generate large test fixtures
552	bun run tests/fixtures/generate-large-outlines.ts
553	
554	# Run benchmarks regularly
555	bun test tests/integration/performance.test.ts
556	```
557	
558	### 6. Use Auto-Save Wisely
559	
560	**Why**: Frequent writes can wear SSDs, but users expect safety.
561	
562	**How**:
563	- Debounce saves (2 seconds of inactivity)
564	- Don't save on every keystroke
565	- Validate before auto-save
566	- Provide manual save option (`Ctrl+S`)
567	
568	### 7. Provide Clear Error Messages
569	
570	**Why**: TUI users can't click "report bug" easily.
571	
572	**How**:
573	```typescript
574	// ❌ Bad
575	throw new Error('Failed');
576	
577	// ✅ Good
578	throw new Error(
579	  `Failed to save outline to ${filePath}: ${error.message}. ` +
580	  `Ensure file is writable and disk has space. ` +
581	  `Your changes are preserved in memory.`
582	);
583	```
584	
585	### 8. Document Keyboard Shortcuts
586	
587	**Why**: TUI discoverability is low - users need reference.
588	
589	**How**:
590	- In-app help screen (Phase 2)
591	- README with full shortcut table
592	- Status bar hints for current mode
593	
594	---
595	
596	## Recommendations
597	
598	### For Future OpenTUI Projects
599	
600	1. **Start with specification**: Define user stories before code
601	2. **Build core logic first**: Pure functions, then UI
602	3. **Test incrementally**: Unit tests before integration tests
603	4. **Profile early**: Performance benchmarks from day one
604	5. **Plan for terminal variance**: Multiple key bindings, test in 3+ terminals
605	6. **Embrace immutability**: Makes state predictable and testable
606	7. **Document as you go**: Record design decisions immediately
607	
608	### Tech Stack Suggestions
609	
610	**Good Fit for OpenTUI**:
611	- Keyboard-first applications (editors, file browsers, task managers)
612	- Data-heavy UIs (logs, tables, trees)
613	- Developer tools (CLIs, debuggers, profilers)
614	- System monitoring dashboards
615	
616	**Poor Fit for OpenTUI**:
617	- Mouse-heavy applications (drawing apps, drag-and-drop)
618	- Rich text editing (formatting, embedded images)
619	- Apps requiring web integration (OAuth flows, embedded browsers)
620	
621	### Performance Targets
622	
623	For similar outliner/tree-view apps:
624	
625	| Operation          | Target | Our Result |
626	| ------------------ | ------ | ---------- |
627	| Keystroke response | <16ms  | <1ms ✅     |
628	| Render 100 nodes   | <50ms  | <1ms ✅     |
629	| Save 1000 nodes    | <100ms | <50ms ✅    |
630	| Load 1000 nodes    | <200ms | <100ms ✅   |
631	
632	These targets are achievable with proper architecture (pure functions, memoization, immutable state).
633	
634	---
635	
636	## Open Questions for OpenTUI Maintainers
637	
638	Issues to potentially file upstream:
639	
640	1. **Mouse event support**: Any plans to expose mouse clicks/scrolling?
641	2. **Multi-line text input**: Is there a pattern for rich text editing?
642	3. **Accessibility**: ARIA-style semantics for screen readers?
643	4. **Styling API**: More CSS-like properties (background gradients, borders)?
644	5. **Performance monitoring**: Built-in profiling hooks?
645	
646	---
647	
648	## Conclusion
649	
650	OpenTUI is a **solid foundation for keyboard-first TUIs** with excellent performance and a familiar React API. The main limitations (no mouse, single-line input) are manageable with good UX design. 
651	
652	**Key Takeaways**:
653	- ✅ Performance targets easily met (<1ms navigation, <100ms file I/O)
654	- ✅ React patterns work well (hooks, memoization, immutability)
655	- ✅ Testing is straightforward (pure functions + integration tests)
656	- ⚠️ Terminal compatibility requires multiple key bindings
657	- ⚠️ Limited documentation - expect to read source code
658	
659	**Would we use OpenTUI again?** Yes. For keyboard-first, data-centric TUIs, it's the best option in the TypeScript ecosystem.
660
