## Addendum: the universal isomorphic outline view (post-alignment)

The 2026-07-12 alignment (see [04-open-questions.md](04-open-questions.md), Q2) widened the
document model from "lists" to the **full markdown block tree** — headings, paragraphs, code
fences, quotes, tables *and* list items are all nodes; the outline is a toggleable editor UI
over any note, with a lossless (isomorphic) round-trip. Feasibility notes for that vision:

- **The parse already exists.** `CachedMetadata.sections` (typed top-level blocks with
  positions), `headings` (text + level + position) and `listItems` (span + parent pointer)
  together describe exactly this block tree for every file in the vault, maintained by core.
  The editor side can parse incrementally from the CM6 document (and/or the bundled
  `@codemirror/language` syntax tree) for keystroke-latency accuracy; the cache serves
  cross-file features.
- **The CM6 architecture is unchanged.** Enforcement (`transactionFilter`), grammar (keymaps),
  node chrome (decorations), zoom (replace-decorations) apply to heading/paragraph nodes the
  same way they apply to list items. Obsidian's own fold system already treats *both* headings
  and list indents as foldable trees — precedent inside the core editor itself.
- **The new hard problem is the mapping algebra, not the APIs.** Headings encode depth by
  level, lists by indentation, paragraphs by adjacency — three different depth encodings in one
  tree. Operations must be closed over "trees expressible as natural markdown" (e.g.
  paragraph-under-paragraph has no markdown form → the op must convert, reject, or remap;
  h6 bounds heading depth). This is a spec/design problem — precisely what the OpenSpec
  explore/design phase is for — not an API gap.
- **Prior art**: org-mode is the closest living system (heading tree + plain lists with
  subtree promote/demote/move); no Obsidian plugin attempts the unified mapping. Obsidian's
  own "fold anything" + `sections` cache suggest the platform was shaped by adjacent thinking.

## Bottom line

content.text:
Research and setup are complete. Everything is documented in [docs/research/](docs/research/README.md) (four docs + index), your four decisions are recorded in [04-open-questions.md](docs/research/04-open-questions.md), and I seeded [openspec/config.yaml](openspec/config.yaml) with the project context so future OpenSpec artifacts inherit it.

**The headline finding: the experience you want is feasible on 100% public APIs.** Obsidian gives us more outliner primitives than expected: the entire CodeMirror 6 extension surface is official API (`registerEditorExtension` — transaction filters can enforce structure, keymaps handle the grammar, decorations handle zoom/node chrome), the metadata cache **already parses every file's block tree** (`sections` + `headings` + `listItems` with parent pointers — the raw material for both your universal mapping and structured backlinks), and block IDs/links/embeds (`^id`, `[[note#^id]]`) are native. Existing plugins collectively de-risk nearly every primitive — obsidian-zoom proves zoom via decorations, and [obsidian-pro-outliner](https://github.com/mrkhachaturov/obsidian-pro-outliner) already ships breadcrumbed zoom, Tana-style mirrors, and Workflowy-style selection expansion. The one thing that is *not* cleanly feasible is the Kanban-style custom-view takeover of `.md` files (requires monkey-patching `WorkspaceLeaf.setViewState`) — which your architecture decision avoids. Also relevant: your "perfect scorecard" goal is now a concrete target — Obsidian's 2026 [community directory](https://obsidian.md/blog/future-of-plugins/) scans every release and shows safety scorecards, with an official [eslint-plugin](https://github.com/obsidianmd/eslint-plugin) to check compliance locally.

Two cautionary tales shaped the recommendations you confirmed: Logseq's in-file metadata pollution (`id::`, `collapsed::`) and its 2026 retreat from file-based graphs, versus outl's clean sidecar approach — hence `^block-id` on demand + folds in plugin data.

**Your Q2 answer changes the project's shape in the best way.** "Universal isomorphic outline view" — headings/paragraphs/blocks as nodes, toggleable UI, lossless round-trip — is genuinely novel (org-mode is the closest prior art; no Obsidian plugin attempts it). I've documented it as the core vision. The APIs support it; the hard problem is the **mapping algebra**, and that's where the remaining ambiguity lives. The sharpest open questions, now listed under Q2 follow-ups:

1. **Paragraph under paragraph** has no markdown form — indenting one paragraph under another must either auto-convert to list items, be rejected, or remap somehow. Which?
2. **Heading nodes** — does indent/outdent change heading level (h2 ↔ h3)? What about skipped levels and the h6 ceiling?
3. **Toggle persistence** — is outline mode per note remembered, or session-only?

Plus the smaller open items (Q5–Q12): fresh build vs vendoring MIT code from outliner/zoom, mobile-from-day-1 vs desktop-first, vim-mode support (its only known implementation uses an undocumented global — tension with the clean-API bar), undo scope, and naming.

Natural next step when you're ready: `/opsx:explore` to work through the mapping algebra — it's the load-bearing design problem for the v1 core — or just answer any of the open questions here and I'll fold them into the log.

While you were researching existing plugins, have you checked this one? https://github.com/springrain1/workflowy-style-outline
Judging by its readme, it has quite some overlap and similar ideas (e.g. the mode toggle and conversion between markdown and outline). Check the docs and the code and let's see what we can learn from it that might be relevant to our project
Good catch — let me clone it and inspect the docs and code directly.

1	# Obsidian Workflowy Plugin
2	
3	A powerful Obsidian plugin that transforms your Markdown notes into a Workflowy-style outline editor with seamless block-level editing capabilities.
4	
5	[中文文档](README-zh_cn.md)
6	
7	## ✨ Features
8	
9	### 🎯 Core Features
10	- **Dual-Mode Editing**: Seamlessly switch between traditional Markdown editing and Workflowy-style outline editing
11	- **Block-Level Operations**: Hierarchical structure with intuitive block manipulation and atomic undo/redo
12	- **Anti-Race Condition Sync**: Bulletproof save queue enforces stable real-time synchronization under high-frequency typing
13	- **Zero Interference**: 5-layer isolation architecture ensures no impact on native Obsidian Markdown experience
14	- **Daily Notes Plus** ⭐: Brand new Daily Notes aggregated outline view (license required, 7-day trial)
15	
16	### 📅 Daily Notes Plus Features (New)
17	- **Aggregated Outline View**: Display and edit multiple Daily Notes in a single unified view
18	- **Flexible Filtering**:
19	  - Note sources: Daily Notes mode / Folder mode / Tag mode
20	  - Time ranges: this week/month/year, last week/month/year, this/last quarter, custom
21	  - Sorting: date/creation time/modification time/file name (ascending/descending)
22	- **Efficient Editing**:
23	  - Each Daily Note as independent section with collapse/expand
24	  - Click title to open as standalone outline view
25	  - Zoom navigation: click bullet to focus on child content with breadcrumb navigation
26	  - Cross-section block dragging (normal drag or Alt+drag for block references)
27	- **Smart Optimization**:
28	  - On-demand loading: configurable initial batch size, dynamic mount on scroll
29	  - Delayed unloading: prevent frequent re-renders when scrolling
30	- **Quick Actions**:
31	  - Auto-create today's note (toolbar button + optional startup creation)
32	  - Date picker for quick navigation
33	  - Preset management for common configurations
34	
35	### ⌨️ Keyboard Shortcuts
36	| Shortcut | Action |
37	|----------|--------|
38	| `Enter` | Create new block |
39	| `Backspace` | Delete empty block / Merge blocks |
40	| `Delete` | Delete character / Merge with next block |
41	| `Tab` / `Shift+Tab` | Indent / Outdent |
42	| `Ctrl+Shift+↑/↓` | Move block up/down |
43	| `Alt+Enter` | Toggle collapse/expand |
44	| `Shift+Enter` | Line break within block |
45	| `Ctrl+Enter` | Toggle todo status |
46	| `Ctrl+Z` | Undo |
47	| `Ctrl+Y` / `Ctrl+Shift+Z` | Redo |
48	| `Ctrl+Backspace` | Clear block content |
49	| `Ctrl+Shift+Backspace` | Delete current block |
50	| `Alt+↑/↓` | Zoom out / Zoom in |
51	| `↑/↓` | Navigate between blocks |
52	| `/` | Open slash command menu |
53	| `#` | Open tag suggestion menu |
54	| `Ctrl+Scroll` | Adjust font size (10-30px) |
55	| `Escape` | Cancel multi-selection / Close menu |
56	| `Ctrl+A` | Select all blocks (in multi-selection mode) |
57	
58	### 🎨 UI Features
59	- **Workflowy-Style Interface**: Clean bullet points with hierarchical indentation lines
60	- **Collapse/Expand**: Hide/show child blocks (click triangle, vertical line, or press `Alt+Enter`)
61	- **Zoom Focus**: Click bullet to zoom into a specific block for focused editing
62	- **Real-Time Search**: Quick search with highlighting (highlight or filter mode)
63	- **Drag & Drop**: Intuitive block reordering via drag and drop
64	- **Multi-Selection**: Select multiple blocks for batch operations (via left-click drag or Ctrl+click)
65	- **Theme Support**: Multiple built-in themes with light/dark mode support
66	- **Responsive Design**: Works perfectly on desktop and mobile devices
67	
68	### 📱 Mobile Features
69	- **Dual-Mode Toolbar**: Edit toolbar (keyboard up) with undo/redo, formatting, insert links/tags/attachments; Function toolbar (keyboard down) with render mode toggle, expand/collapse all, level-based expand
70	- **IME Flicker Prevention**: Local DOM patching avoids full re-render, keeping the soft keyboard stable
71	- **Touch Optimized**: Swipe-to-scroll toolbar, 8px swipe threshold prevents accidental taps, pointerdown focus protection
72	- **Insert Attachment**: Opens system file picker (photos/files), saves to vault and inserts Markdown link
73	
74	### 📝 Editing Modes
75	- **Source Mode**: Direct text editing with Markdown syntax
76	- **Live Preview Mode**: Real-time Markdown rendering with WYSIWYG editing
77	  - Full Markdown formatting support (bold, italic, links, headings, etc.)
78	  - Internal link preview and navigation
79	  - Embed support (images, notes, blocks)
80	  - Code syntax highlighting
81	
82	### ⚡ Slash Command Menu (New)
83	Type `/` to trigger the slash command menu for quick insertion:
84	
85	| Command Type | Function |
86	|--------------|----------|
87	| **Time** | Set task time `[HH:MM]`, supports time ranges (TickTickSync compatible) |
88	| **Priority** | 🔺Highest / ⏫High / 🔼Medium / 🔽Low / ⏬Lowest (obsidian-tasks compatible) |
89	| **Date** | 📅Due / ⏳Scheduled / 🛫Start / ➕Created / ✅Done |
90	| **Status** | ☐Todo / ◐In Progress / ☑Done / ⊘Cancelled / •Normal |

92	### 🏷️ Tag Suggestions (New)
93	Type `#` to trigger the tag suggestion menu:

99	### ✅ Multi-Status Todo Support (New)
100	Supports four todo states, compatible with obsidian-tasks plugin:

104	- `[-]` Cancelled

106	### 🔗 Link Support
107	- Internal links (`[[note]]`) with click navigation
108	- Heading links (`[[note#heading]]`) with scroll and highlight
109	- Block references (`[[note#^blockid]]`) with precise navigation
110	- Embed links (`![[note]]`) with click-to-navigate button
111	- Ctrl+hover for preview popup

115	### 📎 Link Suggestions
116	Type `[[` or `![[` to trigger link suggestions:
117	- Auto-search files in your Vault

122	### 🔀 Cross-Document Drag & Drop
123	- **Normal drag**: Move blocks between documents
124	- **Alt+drag**: Create block references (`![[file#^blockid]]`)
125	- Auto-generates Obsidian-compliant block IDs
126	- Supports multi-select batch dragging
127	- Supports cross-section dragging in Daily Notes view

129	### 📝 Thino Compatibility (New)
130	Compatible with Thino/Memos plugin format:
131	- **Tab Indentation Support**: First-level list items support Tab-indented continuation lines
132	- **Auto Timestamp**: Automatically add timestamps when creating new blocks
133	- **Timestamp Format**: Choose between `HH:mm` or `HH:mm:ss` format
134	- **Seamless Switching**: Preserves Tab indentation when switching between Markdown and Outline views

136	### ✍️ Live Preview Editor Enhancements
137	Markdown shortcuts in Live Preview mode:
138	| Shortcut | Action |
139	|----------|--------|
140	| `Ctrl+B` | Bold |
141	| `Ctrl+I` | Italic |

159	- **Drag files**: Drag files into editor to auto-insert links

164	## 📖 Usage
165	
166	### Switching Views
167	**Switch to Workflowy View:**
168	1. Open any Markdown file
169	2. Use Command Palette (`Ctrl/Cmd+P`) and search "Toggle Outline/Markdown View"
170	3. Or right-click the file and select "Open as Outline Note"
171	
172	**Switch back to Markdown View:**
173	1. In Workflowy view, use Command Palette and search "Toggle Outline/Markdown View"
174	2. Or right-click and select "Open as Markdown"
175	
176	### Basic Operations
177	- **Create Block**: Press `Enter` to create a new block after current one
178	- **Indent Management**: Use `Tab` and `Shift+Tab` to adjust hierarchy
179	- **Move Blocks**: Use `Ctrl+Shift+↑/↓` or drag and drop
180	- **Collapse/Expand**: Click the triangle icon, the vertical indentation line, or press `Alt+Enter`
181	- **Zoom Focus**: Click the bullet point to zoom into that block
182	- **Multi-Select**: Drag up/down with left mouse button, or hold `Ctrl` and click multiple blocks for batch operations
183	
184	### Search
185	- Click the search icon or use the search bar at the top
186	- **Highlight Mode**: Highlights matching content while showing all blocks
187	- **Filter Mode**: Shows only blocks containing the search term
188	- Case-sensitive search option available

190	## 📦 Installation
191	
192	
193	### Manual Installation
194	1. Download the latest release from [Releases](https://github.com/user/obsidian-workflowy-plugin/releases)
195	2. Extract to your Obsidian plugins folder: `{vault}/.obsidian/plugins/obsidian-workflowy-plugin/`
196	3. Enable the plugin in Obsidian settings
197	
198	### Using BRAT

### [workflowy-style-outline](https://github.com/springrain1/workflowy-style-outline) (springrain1) — the Option B case study

*(Added 2026-07-13 — reviewed after the architecture decisions; it independently attempts our
"mode toggle + md↔outline conversion" idea via the custom-view route, so it doubles as a field
test of the road we didn't take.)*

**What it is.** A Workflowy-style block editor for markdown notes (v2.9.1, ~10k lines,
squash-committed as a "fix all Obsidian review issues" clean drop; README additionally
advertises license-gated features not present in this snapshot). Rich feature set: per-note
toggle to an outline view, zoom + breadcrumbs, multi-selection, drag-and-drop (Alt+drag creates
block references with auto-generated native `^block-id`s), slash/tag/link suggestion menus,
todo states, mobile toolbar.

**How it works** (verified in source):

- `WorkflowyView extends FileView` — a **from-scratch block editor**: each node is a raw
  `textarea` (edit layer) over a `contenteditable`/`MarkdownRenderer` display layer. Not CM6.
- Explicit per-note toggle via command/file menu calling **`leaf.setViewState({type: ...})` on
  the active leaf — public API, no monkey-patching**. (Automatic association is simply not
  offered; a leaf left in outline view is restored by workspace persistence.)
- `OutlineParser` does bidirectional md↔tree conversion; file I/O is manual
  `vault.read`/`vault.modify` plus a hand-rolled "anti-race-condition save queue".
- A five-module "isolation architecture" (view-state manager, command proxy, event delegator,
  runtime validator with periodic health checks) exists solely to keep the parallel editor
  from interfering with native Obsidian.

**What its costs demonstrate** (each one a predicted consequence of the custom-view route):

- **The conversion is lossy and normalizing — the anti-example for our isomorphism
  requirement.** The line-regex parser: forces tab indentation and `- ` markers on rewrite
  (ordered lists are parsed but re-serialized as bullets), globs *all* consecutive non-list
  content (headings, paragraphs, fences, tables) into single opaque blob nodes, misparses
  list-looking lines inside code fences, and `.trim()`s away blank-line structure. Toggling a
  note in and out of outline view can rewrite the file.
- **Rebuilding the editor means rebuilding the platform**: the README's proudest features —
  save queue, IME-flicker prevention, incremental mobile DOM patcher, undo manager — are all
  re-implementations of things CM6/Obsidian provide for free inside the markdown view.
- **Non-list content is second-class**: rendered blobs, not first-class nodes — the exact
  compromise our universal mapping (headings/paragraphs as real nodes) is designed to avoid.
- Guideline frictions typical of the approach: manual `vault.modify` of the open file,
  deprecated `workspace.activeLeaf` usage.

**What's genuinely worth learning from it:**

1. **Explicit toggle needs no hacks** — `setViewState` to a registered view type on user action
   is clean public API; only *automatic* md→custom-view association requires the Kanban
   monkey-patch. (Feasibility doc updated accordingly.)
2. **Alt+drag → block reference with on-demand `^block-id`** is a lovely interaction that fits
   our Q3 decision exactly.
3. **Aggregated multi-file outline view** (their "Daily Notes Plus": several notes composed
   into one editable outline with lazy mount/unmount) is a compelling future layer — and
   easier for us, since our nodes stay in the real editor.
4. Their per-block suggestion menus (slash, `#`, `[[`) confirm users expect Obsidian's
   full input affordances *inside* outline nodes — which the editor-centric path keeps for free.

## Backlinks-side plugins ("bi-directional outlining")

content.text:
Yes, that plugin is highly relevant — I cloned it and read the code (~10k lines, v2.9.1). It's essentially a field test of **the exact architecture we decided against** (Option B, custom view), attempting our same ideas: per-note toggle and bidirectional markdown↔outline conversion. I've added a case-study entry to [02-obsidian-plugin-landscape.md](docs/research/02-obsidian-plugin-landscape.md) and one genuine correction to [03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md](docs/research/03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md).

**How it works:** `WorkflowyView extends FileView` — a from-scratch block editor where every node is a raw `textarea` over a `contenteditable`/`MarkdownRenderer` display layer (not CodeMirror). An `OutlineParser` converts md↔tree; saving is manual `vault.read`/`vault.modify` with a hand-rolled "anti-race-condition save queue". A five-module "isolation architecture" (command proxy, event delegator, runtime validator with periodic health checks) exists purely to stop the parallel editor from interfering with native Obsidian.

**One real correction to our research:** its toggle uses **no monkey-patching**. An explicit command/menu action calling `leaf.setViewState({type: ...})` is plain public API — the Kanban hack is only needed for *automatic* md→custom-view association. So a scorecard-clean custom view is more reachable than my feasibility doc originally stated. I've updated the Option B verdict with this nuance.

**Everything else validates our decisions, point by point:**

- **The conversion is lossy — the living anti-example for our isomorphism requirement.** The line-regex parser forces tab indentation and `- ` markers on rewrite (ordered lists come back as bullets), globs all consecutive non-list content — headings, paragraphs, code fences, tables — into single opaque blob nodes, misparses list-looking lines *inside* code fences, and trims away blank-line structure. Toggling a note in and out can rewrite your file. Our property-tested byte-identity round-trip in the `mapping-core` change is precisely the differentiator.
- **Non-list content is second-class** (read-mostly rendered blobs), whereas our universal mapping makes headings and paragraphs first-class nodes.
- **Leaving the platform's editor means rebuilding the platform.** Their proudest README features — save queue, IME-flicker prevention, incremental mobile DOM patcher, custom undo manager — are all re-implementations of what CM6 inside the markdown view gives us for free. The isolation layer is self-inflicted complexity our editor-centric path never needs.

**Worth stealing:** Alt+drag a block to another note to create a block reference with an auto-generated native `^block-id` (fits our on-demand-ID decision perfectly); their license-gated "Daily Notes Plus" — several notes aggregated into one editable outline with lazy mounting — is a compelling future layer, and easier on our architecture since nodes stay in real editors; and their slash/`#`/`[[` menus confirm users expect full Obsidian input affordances inside nodes — which we inherit for free.

One caveat for anyone evaluating it as a user: the public repo is a single squashed "fix all Obsidian review issues" commit, and the README advertises paid, license-gated features not present in the published source.

Compliance is checkable locally via the official
[`obsidianmd/eslint-plugin`](https://github.com/obsidianmd/eslint-plugin).

**What the scorecard empirically measures** (decomposed from a real red one — see the
workflowy-style-outline case study in the plugin-landscape doc). Two independent badges:
*Health* (activity: readme/license hygiene, commit/release cadence, issue responsiveness,
adoption) and *Review* (automated scans of each release). The Review scans are, concretely:
(1) capability detection surfaced as user-facing disclosures — vault read/write, network
calls, clipboard, base64 usage; (2) dependency vulnerability scan; (3) malware/obfuscation/
network-behavior scans gated on **build verification, which requires a committed lockfile**;
(4) the obsidianmd + strict-TypeScript eslint rules; (5) CSS lints (`!important`, `:has`);
(6) release hygiene (GitHub artifact attestations, release notes, manifest/release
consistency). Monkey-patching/private-API use is *not* among the automated checks — it
remains a manual-review and fragility concern, not a scorecard line item.

**Green-scorecard checklist for this project (day 1):** eslint-plugin-obsidianmd + strict
typescript-eslint in CI; zero `!important`/`:has` (style via Obsidian CSS variables and
specificity); committed lockfile + GitHub artifact attestations + real release notes in the
release workflow; no network/clipboard capability at all (vault-only footprint → near-empty

content.text:
I pulled the full scorecard from the directory page (it's client-rendered, so I read it in the browser). Your instinct is right: **the red is almost entirely lint-grade hygiene, not architecture** — and importantly for the monkey-patching reassessment, **the automated scans don't check for monkey-patching or private-API use at all**. Here's the decomposition of the 1,088 flagged issues behind the "Review: Risks" badge:

- **~890 (roughly 86%) are a single CSS rule**: "avoid `!important`" — the predictable cost of their from-scratch UI shipping a huge stylesheet that fights Obsidian's. Plus "avoid `:has`" (performance).
- **The official eslint ruleset + strict TypeScript**: deprecated `activeLeaf` (7×), default hotkeys (10×), `document` vs `activeDocument` (24×), `instanceof` vs `.instanceOf()`, deprecated `execCommand`/`substr`, 44× `any`, unhandled promises, README title not matching the manifest name.
- **Supply-chain/build items** — the most substantive category: no committed lockfile, which cascades into *"build verification not available"*, and therefore **malware, obfuscation, and network-behavior scans all show "not available"** too. Plus missing GitHub artifact attestations, a release-manifest mismatch, and empty release notes.
- **Capability disclosures** (informational, not violations): network request calls, clipboard access, `atob`/`btoa` base64 usage ("may be used to obscure strings") — shown to users regardless.

Meanwhile its *Health* badge is "Excellent" — the two badges measure completely different things (activity vs. scan results).

So the reassessment stands, with sharper edges now: the explicit `setViewState` toggle is public API and wouldn't be flagged by anything here; monkey-patching remains a **manual-review and fragility** concern rather than a scorecard line item. The real lesson is the opposite direction: a green Review badge is cheap **if designed in from day 1** and expensive to retrofit. I've added the scorecard decomposition to the [case study](docs/research/02-obsidian-plugin-landscape.md) and a concrete day-1 checklist to the [feasibility doc](docs/research/03-obsidian-api-feasibility.md): the official eslint plugin + strict TS in CI, zero `!important` (style via Obsidian's CSS variables — another point for the editor-centric path, which barely needs custom CSS), committed lockfile + artifact attestations + real release notes in the release workflow, and a vault-only capability footprint (no network, no clipboard) so our disclosure panel stays near-empty.

One caveat worth keeping in mind: the page itself says scorecards are "work in progress" and may contain errors, so the exact rule set will likely evolve — but the categories (capabilities, build verification, official eslint rules, release hygiene) are clearly the durable shape of it.
